interactions.summary: Jiang begins Jewish history from geography: Jerusalem is the imperial trade hinge that every surrounding empire needs to control. He then narrates Babylonian destruction, Persian divide-and-rule, the emergence of Jewish identity around land, law, and Bible-memory, Hellenistic scattering, and the Maccabean revolt as a minority religious victory against Greek religious absorption.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with brief student readings or checks where noted.

interactions.summary: Jiang moves from Rome’s destruction of the Second Temple to a transformation of Jewish religion: temple worship yields to rabbinic law, some Jews convert into Christianity and Islam, and those who remain in Christian Europe enter an unwritten bargain with nobility as financiers, useful specialists, and scapegoats.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with brief student readings or checks where noted.

interactions.summary: Jiang frames the Spanish expulsion, Polish refuge, and renewed massacres as producing three theological questions: why conversion seems unpunished, why loyal Jews suffer, and why the promised land remains lost. Sabbatai Zevi appears as a messianic revolutionary against rabbinic hierarchy, law, and status quo slavery.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with brief student readings or checks where noted.

interactions.summary: Jiang explains Zevi’s conversion to Islam as sacrificial redemption for converts and as the seed of justification by sin. He then introduces Jacob Frank as Zevi’s reincarnated successor, systematizing Sabbatian heresy into a success religion that teaches followers to break the world’s rules, become kings, and learn through stories.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with brief student readings or checks where noted.

interactions.summary: Students read Frank stories while Jiang interprets them as training in contempt for rigid law. The knife trick and plague story teach that law-bound people are superstitious, manipulable, and already spiritually dead; justification by sin becomes a method for transcending conventional morality and taking power.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with brief student readings or checks where noted.

interactions.summary: A student reads more Frank material and Jiang extracts the theology: if divine figures come to experience everything, rulership should also be an experience; Frank claims Moses, David, Solomon, and Jesus did not open the final door, while his mission is to destroy Satan’s order by sinning against its laws.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with brief student readings or checks where noted.

interactions.summary: Jiang compresses three Frankist parables into a training manual: the pearl rewards fearless action, the dead king story defines internal family loyalty, the bear story says a few brothers bound by love beat thousands, and the elephant story makes love rather than money or power the secret of conquest.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with brief student readings or checks where noted.

interactions.summary: Frank’s stories shift from loyalty to methods of conquest. Jiang says the honest robber shows how good animals become loyal, the wedding-dispute story teaches intellectual attack rather than violence, and the Elohim passage makes flesh and sin the route to knowledge unavailable to gods.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with brief student readings or checks where noted.

interactions.summary: Jiang presents Frankist eschatology as material rather than spiritual: Eva is the female messiah, the end of days means wealth, pleasure, and freedom from death, and the pear-garden story rewrites Eden and Augustine so theft becomes creative boundary-testing rather than original sin.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with brief student readings or checks where noted.

interactions.summary: Jiang turns the pear story into explicit rule theory. God values creativity and testing boundaries; laws, taboos, and transgressions are hallucinated conventions. A student asks when such freedom is possible, and Jiang answers that the mystic who sees reality as imagination becomes king by breaking false limits.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with brief student readings or checks where noted.

interactions.summary: A student reads the roast-goose test, where a generous rich man discovers that his beneficiaries betray him. Jiang interprets it as a hard lesson in wealth: generosity makes others see you as naive, so staying rich requires recognizing people as dangerous animals. The next reading adds that silver answers everything and money is needed even to approach God.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with brief student readings or checks where noted.

interactions.summary: Jiang distinguishes mission from money, then interprets a sexual Frankist story as imagination constructing reality. A student asks how a private inner world makes others follow, and Jiang answers through writers, television, education, dialogue, and debate: trained imagination can pull others into its world. The packet ends with the conversion-night ritual.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with brief student readings or checks where noted.

interactions.summary: Jiang interprets the conversion-night sexual ritual as covenant rather than mere sex: followers swear loyalty to each other before dispersing in disguise through an idiotic world. He then treats wife swapping as women’s empowerment in Frankist egalitarianism, reads Eva-incest material as reincarnation strategy, and compares Catholic works and Lutheran faith to Frankist faith through sin.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with brief student readings or checks where noted.

interactions.summary: Jiang gives the lecture’s organizational thesis: Zevi and Frank turn sin into proof of faith, guilt into energy, and a secret church into a resilient world-conquering organization. Frankism rejects Judaism’s spirit/law orientation for material wisdom, acceleration, and making paradise now, then Jiang links the pattern to Faust, Illuminati, Jesuits, Freemasons, Mormonism, Scientology, Brandeis, Yeats, and modernity.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with brief student readings or checks where noted.

interactions.summary: The class turns to Yeats. A student reads Crazy Jane and the Bishop while Jiang interprets the poem as Frankist: the bishop offers heavenly respectability, but Crazy Jane answers that fair and foul need each other and that bodily lowliness teaches pride.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Students reading quoted Frank/Yeats material with Jiang framing.

interactions.summary: The Yeats reading reaches its thesis: love has pitched its mansion in excrement, so nothing whole exists without being torn. Jiang translates this into Frankist self-destruction and then answers a student: sin is not pleasure for its own sake but a taboo-wall one breaks to gain knowledge through guilt, reflection, pain, and resistance to social conformity. Another student asks about Israel-Palestine, and Jiang says Sabbatean Frankists founded Israel.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with brief student readings or checks where noted.

interactions.summary: The lecture ends abruptly after the Israel-Palestine question. A student acknowledges Jiang’s answer, Jiang begins to say what is happening in Palestine and Israel today, the transcript becomes garbled, and he closes by saying he will see the class next week.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with brief student readings or checks where noted.

speaker_notes.note: Segment 0003 contains ASR corruption around the first pillar, but the surrounding land/law/Bible identity model is clear.
Segments 0033-0038 are student readings from Frank material; Jiang interprets them in segments 0035-0040.
Several read-aloud segments have heavy ASR damage, but Jiang’s interpretation in adjacent segments clarifies the theological claims.
Segment 0061 appears to contain ASR damage around Warsaw lawyers, but the surrounding claim is that Frankists become powerful through law and scholarship.
Segments 0065-0071 are read-aloud Frank material; Jiang’s interpretation follows in 0066-0072.
Segment 0075 is a substantive student question; segment 0076 is Jiang’s answer.
Segments 0081-0086 and 0088 are student readings from Frank material; Jiang interprets the story in 0082, 0084, and 0087.
Segments 0090 and 0096 contain explicit quoted sexual material read by students; Jiang interprets them as ritual and imagination rather than treating them as public questions.
Student-read quoted material contains explicit sexual and incest references; Jiang frames these as ritual, covenant, and reincarnation theology.
Claims about Frankist control or membership in later institutions are presented by Jiang as rumor/speculation in places and should remain attributed to this lecture.
Segments 0114, 0116, and 0118 are student readings of Yeats; Jiang interprets in 0117 and 0119.
Segments 0124 and 0127 are substantive student questions; 0125-0126 and 0128 are Jiang’s answers.
Segments 0130-0132 are heavily ASR-damaged; do not use them as firm evidence except for the visible closeout.

claims.claim: Jerusalem is framed as the center of the ancient world because it links Egypt, Anatolia, Mesopotamia, Persia, and the Mediterranean trade routes.
The Persians are said to solve the Jerusalem problem by financing a Jewish identity implanted in the Levant as a loyal foreign entity.
Jiang defines early Jewish identity through the promised land, the Law of Moses, and the Bible as literal collective memory.
The Maccabean revolt is presented as a minority religious rebellion whose success becomes Hanukkah.
The Roman destruction of the Second Temple is treated as the event that shifts Judaism from temple sacrifice to rabbinic law.
Christianity and Islam are described as paths by which many Jews remained connected to Jewish messianic hopes while adapting to Roman or Arabian worlds.
In Catholic Europe, Jiang says Jews occupy an unwritten contract: useful for finance and trade but retained as scapegoats when crisis comes.
Muslim Spain is contrasted with Catholic Europe as a place where Jews prosper because Abrahamic kinship moderates persecution.
The 1492 Spanish expulsion is presented as a traumatic rupture that creates bitter division between converts and non-converts.
The Polish-Lithuanian refuge initially offers tolerance but becomes another site of Jewish insecurity through invasion and war.
Jiang says Jewish persecution creates three crisis questions about conversion, unjust suffering, and the absent promised land.
Sabbatai Zevi is framed as a messianic revolutionary who attacks rabbinic hierarchy and replaces law with direct faith, equality, and freedom.
Zevi’s conversion is interpreted as a messianic sacrifice redeeming Jews from the sin of conversion and making the messianic age internal to believers.
Justification by sin means breaking rabbinic law can show true faith in God, the Messiah, and the self rather than mere permission to misbehave.
Jiang claims the Donmeh families who followed Zevi eventually controlled Turkey, naming Mustafa Ataturk as Donmeh.
Jacob Frank is introduced as Zevi’s reincarnated successor who turns the movement into a worldly success religion against Judaism.
Frank’s knife story is read as a lesson that rigid law makes learned people stupid and vulnerable to manipulation.
The plague story is interpreted as seeing the world as a corpse: if others are already spiritually dead, the initiate may take from them and triumph.
Jiang says justification by sin proves faith by breaking the laws of conventional man rather than obeying social morality.
Frank’s critique of suffering asks why divine beings experience death but not kingship, making rulership part of wisdom.
Jiang says Frank portrays Moses, David, Solomon, and prior religious pillars as slaves who could not see or conquer the true structure of the world.
Frank’s Jesus-water dream is interpreted as a claim that life and messianic authority flow away from Jesus toward Frank.
Sinning is framed as defying Satan’s false laws, revealing the world’s falseness, and accelerating the end of this world.
The pearl parable teaches that fearlessness and refusal to obey convention are what make a person wealthy and powerful.
The dead king story distinguishes outsiders as animals from the company as family, bound by love and respect even in death.
The bear story teaches that a few brothers united by love are more powerful than many enemies.
The elephant-prince story makes love, not money or power, the motive that lets the prince fulfill an impossible promise.
The released robber story is interpreted as a lesson that good animals become loyal when treated well.
The wedding-dispute story teaches followers to win through argument and learning by exposing contradictions in sacred law.
Jiang says Frankist education pushes followers toward law, scholarship, and critical argument as routes to power.
The Elohim passage is interpreted as a theology where flesh and sin give mortals access to knowledge gods cannot obtain.
Jiang says the Messiah in Frankism is Jacob Frank’s daughter Eva, making the messianic figure female.
Frankist eschatology is described as material pleasure and immortality rather than disembodied spirit.
The goddess-killing-children story teaches followers not to question Frank because he claims access to dimensions and purposes they cannot see.
The pear-garden story rewrites Eden and Augustine by saying God wants rule-breaking creativity that increases wealth.
Jiang says Frank teaches that God will not punish creative boundary-testing as long as one does not violate fundamental harms like killing or hurting people.
A student asks whether there is a specific time when people can act freely without legal restraint.
Jiang answers that in Kabbalistic mysticism reality is consciousness or hallucination, so social laws and taboos are made-up limits rather than reality itself.
Frank’s anti-rabbinic line is interpreted as a command to think for yourself rather than obey human rabbis.
The roast-goose story tests whether beneficiaries love a rich man or only exploit his generosity.
Jiang interprets the story to mean that kindness from the wealthy is read as stupidity and invites plotting.
He says becoming rich requires breaking man-made laws, while staying rich requires meanness and a low view of human nature.
The quoted line that silver answers everything makes money a spiritual as well as practical medium.
Jiang says Frankist success is measured by mission rather than money: the mission is to create a new world and end the age.
The nun story is interpreted as reality being constructed through imagination; Frank changes the world by imagining and acting inside his own reality.
A student asks how a private imagined world can make others follow if it is not communicated or enacted.
Jiang answers that powerful imagination works like writing or television: trained through education, reading, dialogue, and debate, it brings others into a created world.
The conversion-night reading introduces a ritual of nakedness, cross-kissing, and ordered sexual relations before mass Catholic conversion.
Jiang says the conversion ritual is a covenant of love that binds followers to each other before they convert and disperse.
Frankist disguise is necessary because the world is run by idiots and governed by idiotic laws, but insiders can recognize one another through covenant.
Jiang provocatively interprets Frankist wife swapping as women gaining access to powerful men and therefore a form of empowerment.
The Eva incest passage is interpreted as an attempt to create another body for Frank’s reincarnation.
Jiang contrasts Catholic justification by works and Lutheran justification by faith before asking how one can prove true belief.
Zevi and Frank say sin proves true faith because being hated and shunned for transgression intensifies belief.
Jiang defines the strongest organization through hierarchy, purpose, selflessness, love, money, secrecy, discipline, energy, and negative emotions generated by sin.
Frankism is distinguished from Judaism as material rather than spiritual: wisdom comes through matter and sin, not obedience to Yahweh.
Frankist acceleration rejects waiting for the Messiah and instead seeks to create paradise by seizing the day and exposing the world’s falseness.
Jiang speculates that Frankist philosophy influenced the Illuminati, Faust, Jesuits, Freemasons, Mormonism, Scientology, Brandeis, Yeats, and modern Western culture.
Jiang introduces Yeats as a Nobel poet and member of the Order of the Golden Dawn, then uses Crazy Jane Talks with a Bishop to show Frankist influence on modernity.
The bishop’s speech is interpreted as conventional religious salvation: leave the foul body and live in a heavenly mansion.
Crazy Jane’s answer is read as Frankist because fair and foul require each other and bodily lowliness produces knowledge and pride.
Jiang interprets Yeats’s excrement line as saying God and love are found in garbage, descent, and self-destruction.
He claims Frankist belief has conquered modern Western culture, to be shown in the next class.
A student asks whether sin is a low vibration needed for learning mistakes and success.
Jiang answers that sin is taboo or invisible walls; it reveals reality, the self, guilt, reflection, wisdom, pain, and freedom from social conformity.
A student asks whether Frankist ideology affects the Palestine-Israel conflict, and Jiang answers that Sabbatean Frankists are the founders of Israel in his course framing.
The closing exchange continues the previous question about Israel-Palestine but the transcript becomes too garbled to extract a reliable substantive claim beyond Jiang’s earlier answer.
Jiang ends the session by deferring continuation to the next week.

signature_moments.moment: All roads lead to Jerusalem because whoever controls it controls the empire’s most profitable hinge.
signature_moments.source_phrase: all roads lead to Jerusalem
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This gives the lecture its geopolitical engine: religion and identity are built around a strategic choke point.

signature_moments.moment: A minority wins because religious conviction turns political weakness into force.
signature_moments.source_phrase: because of their religious conviction, because of the will of God, they will triumph
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It sets up the later problem of faith as a power technology.

signature_moments.moment: The house of God burns, and the religion moves from temple to law.
signature_moments.source_phrase: gone from temple worship to worship of their law
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the institutional hinge from priests to rabbis.

signature_moments.moment: The European bargain is brutal: make money for us until we need someone to blame.
signature_moments.source_phrase: when we need a scapegoat, you get blamed and you have to leave
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This gives the later crisis of faith its material and moral pressure.

signature_moments.moment: The crisis has three questions: why conversion, why punishment, why no promised land?
signature_moments.source_phrase: So why is this happening? This creates a crisis of faith.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns history into theology and makes messianic revolt intelligible.

signature_moments.moment: Zevi turns messiah into revolutionary: the enemy is not Rome but the religious hierarchy keeping Jews obedient.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Whenever you have a Messiah come... he is essentially a revolutionary
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This prepares Frank’s later attack on law as control.

signature_moments.moment: The messianic age has come because you are the messianic age now.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you are the messianic age now
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the interiorization that lets law collapse into self-authorized faith.

signature_moments.moment: Stories are seeds that grow into oak trees inside the mind.
signature_moments.source_phrase: stories are... like seeds... they become like oak trees
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is Jiang’s education theory for why Frank teaches through parables.

signature_moments.moment: Their learning has made them stupid.
signature_moments.source_phrase: their laws have made them stupid
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The polemic converts education into a liability when it is only obedience to rules.

signature_moments.moment: See the world as a corpse and you can stand above people.
signature_moments.source_phrase: see the world as a corpse
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the lecture’s darkest image of transgressive sovereignty.

signature_moments.moment: Why did the divine taste death but not rulership?
signature_moments.source_phrase: why then did it not taste... rulership
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The question flips holiness from suffering into sovereignty.

signature_moments.moment: Sin becomes acceleration: break Satan’s laws to end Satan’s world.
signature_moments.source_phrase: by sinning you accelerate the end of the world
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the clearest statement of the lecture’s accelerationist theology.

signature_moments.moment: If there is a pearl, puncture it; fearlessness gets paid.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Just go do it, man. Seize it
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives Frankism a practical psychology of risk.

signature_moments.moment: Four brothers are more powerful than ten thousand men.
signature_moments.source_phrase: four brothers are more powerful than 10,000 men
signature_moments.why_it_matters: Love becomes not sentiment but organizational force.

signature_moments.moment: Do not beat everyone up; win by dispute.
signature_moments.source_phrase: not... by fighting, but by disputation
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The movement’s conquest method becomes intellectual and legal rather than only violent.

signature_moments.moment: Because we can sin, we can learn what gods cannot.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Because we can sin, we can learn knowledge in a way that the gods cannot
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is Frankist anthropology in one line: flesh is not weakness but epistemic privilege.

signature_moments.moment: Why be a spirit in space when true pleasure is food, sex, drink, wealth, and no death?
signature_moments.source_phrase: True pleasure is to enjoy life
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It reverses spiritual salvation into material immortality.

signature_moments.moment: The pear theft makes Eden God’s fault and Augustine’s guilt stupid.
signature_moments.source_phrase: it is a rewriting of the story of the Garden of Eden
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is a compact example of Frankism rewriting inherited moral origin stories.

signature_moments.moment: Reality is just a hallucination, and the one who knows the laws are fake becomes king.
signature_moments.source_phrase: reality is just a hallucination
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This connects mystical metaphysics to political domination.

signature_moments.moment: If a gold bar sits in front of you, refusing it is not virtue; it is stupidity.
signature_moments.source_phrase: it doesn’t mean you’re virtuous. It just means you’re stupid
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the lecture’s most blunt anti-conventional moral provocation.

signature_moments.moment: Be generous and people will call you naive, then betray you.
signature_moments.source_phrase: they’ll think you’re stupid and naive, and they’ll plot against you
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It hardens the lecture’s power ethic around money and suspicion.

signature_moments.moment: Silver answers everything, even the approach to God.
signature_moments.source_phrase: silver answers everything
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The line fuses material wealth with religious access.

signature_moments.moment: Everyone is living in someone else’s imagination.
signature_moments.source_phrase: everyone is living in someone else’s imagination
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the metaphysical key for the later public read.

signature_moments.moment: A writer imagines a world; when you read it, you enter it.
signature_moments.source_phrase: When you read the book, what happens? You come into this world.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives a non-mystical bridge from imagination to social power.

signature_moments.moment: The sexual ritual is a covenant: go anywhere, hide anywhere, but love will let us find each other.
signature_moments.source_phrase: because of our love for each other, we’ll find each other
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This reframes the movement’s scandalous ritual as organizational glue.

signature_moments.moment: The question after Luther is not what you do, but how you prove that you really believe.
signature_moments.source_phrase: how do you really believe
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It sets up sin as the next escalation of faith.

signature_moments.moment: Guilt from sin becomes organizational fuel: self-hatred, fear, anger, jealousy, energy, motivation.
signature_moments.source_phrase: This guilt can be channeled
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is Jiang’s mechanism for turning transgression into disciplined power.

signature_moments.moment: Do not wait for God; make your own world, your own paradise.
signature_moments.source_phrase: God wants us to make our own world, our own paradise
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the positive program behind the transgression.

signature_moments.moment: Faust becomes Frankish: sin for wisdom, then be saved because wisdom was the point.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Sin as much as you want in order to gain wisdom
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It connects the lecture’s esoteric history to canonical modern literature.

signature_moments.moment: You cannot have good without evil, beauty without ugliness.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Fair and foul are near of kin
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This condenses the Yeats/Frankist reversal of moral purity.

signature_moments.moment: The low body becomes the teacher of pride.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Learned in bodily lowliness and in the heart’s pride
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It links poetic embodiment to Frankist knowledge through descent.

signature_moments.moment: God is in garbage; you have to sink low to find God and yourself.
signature_moments.source_phrase: God is in garbage
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the lecture’s final spiritual reversal.

signature_moments.moment: Sin is not for the sake of sinning; it is a wall-breaking method for deeper knowledge.
signature_moments.source_phrase: don’t sin just for the sake of sinning
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This prevents the public read from flattening Frankism into mere hedonism.

signature_moments.moment: Destroy yourself in order to create yourself.
signature_moments.source_phrase: destroy yourself in order to truly create yourself
signature_moments.why_it_matters: A compact modernity thesis tying Yeats, Frankism, and self-making.

signature_moments.moment: The lecture closes on an unfinished Israel-Palestine bridge, leaving the Frankist-modernity claim for next week.
signature_moments.source_phrase: I’ll see you guys next week
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It marks the episode as part one of a continuing argument.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The trade and worship hinge that empires must control and that Jewish identity later centers on.
A Jewish rebellion against Hellenistic religious absorption, remembered in Hanukkah.
The Jerusalem worship center whose destruction forces a shift in Jewish religious authority.
Jiang’s term-like model for Jews serving nobility economically while remaining blameable in crisis.
A historical-theological breakdown in which persecution makes inherited explanations fail.
The messianic figure Jiang presents as a proto-liberal/proto-communist enemy of rabbinic hierarchy.
The Frankist/Sabbatean idea that transgression can demonstrate true faith and access wisdom.
Families said to convert with Zevi and preserve his teachings in secret.
Zevi’s claimed reincarnated successor and the lecture’s central teacher of worldly conquest.
Jiang’s interpretation of Frank’s view that conventional reality is spiritually dead and exploitable.
Human morality and social law treated as barriers to true faith and power.
The idea that transgression hastens the end of the false world and reveals truth.
Frank’s follower community, described as egalitarian under direct loyalty to Frank.
Frank’s inner family of brothers and sisters, contrasted with outsiders treated as animals.
The binding force Jiang says lets Frankist followers conquer without mere money or coercion.
Rendered as gods in the reading; Jiang uses it to describe a hierarchy of gods and mortal flesh.
Argument used to defeat law-bound opponents and gain status.
Jacob Frank’s daughter, identified here as the female Messiah.
An interdimensional higher god who arrives at the end of days with wealth and freedom from death.
Jiang’s Kabbalistic framing of reality as consciousness and imagination rather than fixed external law.
The shared moral/legal weight that Frankists claim to lift easily once they see through it.
A Frankist money maxim read in class: money is necessary even for approaching God.
Jiang’s harsh Frankist anthropology: people are animals who must be judged as good or bad.
Frankist commitment to creating a new world and ending the age, valued above money.
Imagination strengthened by education, reading, dialogue, and debate until it can shape other people’s realities.
A ritual bond of love and mutual recognition among followers.
The belief that Frank continues through new bodies, tied here to Eva and incestuous reproduction.
Catholic salvation model where doing what the church commands can lead to heaven.
Jiang’s name for Jacob Frank’s anti-Judaic, material, secretive, sin-as-wisdom religious organization.
Goethe play Jiang reads as Frankish: wisdom through sin and final rescue.
Secret-society frame Jiang speculatively links to Frankist coordination.

glossary_terms.term: Crazy Jane Talks with a Bishop
glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Yeats poem Jiang uses to show fair/foul, body/spirit reversals as Frankist in mood.
Secret society Jiang associates with Yeats and the spread of Frankist-style modernity.
Jiang’s closing definition: sin is a socially imagined barrier one breaks for knowledge, guilt, reflection, and wisdom.
The combined lineage Jiang names when answering a question about Israel’s founding.
